summary: This advice column tackles questions about Bitcoin's viability, emotional sensitivity across cultures, New York City travel recommendations, and whether to date investment bankers. On Bitcoin, Aunt Pythia suggests viewing current troubles as growing pains while noting Google's gmail-based payment system as a serious competitor. The investment banking question prompts a nuanced discussion about balancing individual judgment against statistical filtering in online dating. Throughout, she maintains her characteristic blend of practical advice and social commentary.
key_points: Bitcoin's troubles (Mt. Gox bankruptcy, regulatory confusion) are growing pains, but Google's gmail payment plans pose real competition
Being openly emotional is manageable by preemptively warning others rather than explaining yourself mid-tears
Authentic New York sightseeing means walking neighborhoods and finding local spots, not tourist attractions
Investment bankers aren't automatically morally bankrupt, but using job as a filtering criterion in online dating is rational given limited time
Online dating has profoundly changed cultural mating practices in ways deserving serious study
money_quotes: I would go to the bathroom and trade bitcoin on my smartphone and then return to my real desk to do my real job trading real currency.
Instead of paying heavy fees, you – guess what – tell Google about your checking accounts and other financial information.
Official tourist destinations are incredibly boring and expensive for what they offer (and what they offer is bum-bag bearing tourists, which you can already see in NZ anyway).
Never disqualify someone on one attribute, especially if they otherwise seem great and you find yourself liking them at a basic human level.
If you are playing the numbers – which is all you can do on these websites – then you might well decide to avoid investment bankers.

There are plenty of people in finance and even in investment banking that are perfectly nice and even sensitive and empathetic. On the other hand, there is some story explaining why they're there, and it often exposes a weird side to them.
I would love someone to do a quantitative and qualitative investigation to see how people have changed their dating and mating habits through online dating.
key_insight: Individual exceptions don't invalidate statistical reasoning—whether filtering potential dates by profession or evaluating new technologies, playing the numbers while remaining open to surprises is the pragmatic approach.
